claims.claim_text: Tech giants (Google, Amazon, etc.) are capitalist producers engaged in massive investment and production, not parasitic feudal rentiers who 'need not lift a finger.'

The 'rent vs. profit' distinction is systematically misapplied to the digital economy: revenues from automated or data-intensive firms can be capitalist profits (surplus value absorbed from elsewhere in the economy), not necessarily rents from monopoly control.

The state's constitutive role in building and sustaining tech dominance is systematically erased by the techno-feudalist framework; relatedly, 'user-ism' (fixation on data extraction as the principal profit driver) misidentifies the locus of value creation in the digital economy.

Frameworks like Zuboff's 'surveillance capitalism' and Italian 'cognitive capitalism' commit the error of 'user-ism' or 'workerism'—they fixate on data extraction from users/workers while ignoring the actual commodity-production processes (e.g., Google's indexing) that generate the bulk of tech profits.

The unresolved Brenner–Wallerstein debate—whether capitalism is driven solely by exploitation via competitive market dynamics, or also constitutively involves extra-economic expropriation—creates the analytical opening through which the techno-feudalism thesis enters. An expanded conception of capitalism integrating expropriation as constitutive would dissolve the need for feudal analogies.

Capitalism is 'infinitely adaptable' (Braudel) and has always leveraged both economic and extra-economic means; IP rights and intangible assets do not transform capitalist firms into feudal lords. The rent-vs-profit distinction is being misapplied: Marx's own framework treats automated/IP-heavy firms as absorbing surplus value as profit, not rent.
